name: SLB N.V.

profile.description: SLB N.V. (formerly Schlumberger Limited) is the world's largest oilfield services company, operating through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. The company provides technology solutions for energy exploration, production, and management worldwide. SLB has repositioned toward digital solutions and energy transition, including carbon management and data center solutions. Founded in 1926, headquartered in Houston, Texas.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB has executed a meaningful strategic transformation under CEO Olivier Le Peuch — rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB in 2025, acquiring ChampionX to expand into production chemicals and artificial lift, and building a Data Center Solutions business that grew 45% YoY to $141M in Q1 2026 [1]. The pivot from a pure oilfield services company to a technology-driven energy solutions provider represents a genuine business model evolution. AI Deployment (1/2): SLB has embedded AI across its operations including the Delfi cognitive E&P environment, AI-driven subsurface modeling, and digital operations that grew 87% YoY in Q1 2026 [1]. Digital revenue of $640M (up 9% YoY) demonstrates quantifiable AI/digital deployment. Market Share (1/2): SLB is the #1 global oilfield services company by revenue ($36B+), with leading positions in wireline, drilling, and reservoir characterization. However, excluding the ChampionX acquisition, Q1 2026 revenue declined 7% YoY, indicating organic market share pressure [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Data Center Solutions (cooling, power, infrastructure) represents a new growth vector adjacent to energy expertise. Pipeline visibility is limited to general growth targets rather than specific product launches. R&D Intensity (1/1): SLB invests approximately 3-4% of revenue in R&D and technology, which is among the highest absolute levels in the energy services industry at over $1B annually [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): SLB's repositioned purpose — driving energy innovation for a balanced planet — is specific and thoughtful, reflecting Le Peuch's vision of SLB as a 'global technology company' rather than merely an oilfield services firm [2]. The rebranding from Schlumberger and the strategic pivot toward digital and energy transition demonstrate that the purpose shapes strategic decisions. It hasn't yet been validated across multiple leadership transitions to reach the highest tier. Community Impact (1/2): SLB operates sustainability programs across 100+ countries, with specific focus on Scope 3 emissions reduction, water conservation, and community development in operating regions. The company received an A- CDP Climate rating and S&P Sustainability Yearbook inclusion in 2025 [3]. Employee Retention (1/2): Le Peuch has led SLB since 2019, providing stability during a transformative period. The company has 109,000 employees across 100+ countries with a culture emphasizing 'collective creativity' [2]. However, the energy services industry has high cyclical turnover, and SLB reduced headcount during the 2020 downturn. Insider Buying (1/2): Le Peuch maintains significant SLB equity holdings, signaling alignment, but no notable net open-market insider buying was identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored across Dividend stock metrics (dividend yield 2.04% > 2% and 44+ consecutive years paid qualifies SLB for Dividend sleeve). D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target (score 1.0). ROE of 14.1% is below the 15% target (score 0.94, 1x weight). Revenue growth YoY of 2.7% is below the 10% target (score 0.76). Revenue 5yr CAGR of 9.4% is close to the 10% target (score 0.96, 1x weight). Forward P/E of 17.3x is below the 20x target (score 1.0). Profit margin of 9.3% is below the 20% target (score 0.46). Payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target (score 0.82, 1x weight). Consecutive years paid of 44 far exceeds the 10 target (score 1.0, 2x weight). Consecutive years growth of 4 is below target (score 0.40, 1x weight). 5yr dividend growth CAGR of 5.4% is below the 6% target (score 0.90, 2x weight). Weighted sum 10.14/12 yields a score of 8 [4].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): SLB's core business is deeply rooted in physical-world energy operations — drilling wells, characterizing subsurface reservoirs, managing production systems, and deploying specialized equipment in extreme environments globally [1]. These activities require physical infrastructure, certified equipment, trained field engineers, and decades of proprietary subsurface data that AI cannot substitute. AI may actually increase demand for SLB's services as AI data centers drive massive energy consumption growth. The company's new Data Center Solutions business ($141M Q1 revenue, +45% YoY) directly benefits from AI infrastructure buildout [1]. The primary AI risk is that SLB's consulting and technical advisory services could face some competition from AI-enhanced analytics tools. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB's Digital & Integration division generated $640M in Q1 2026 revenue (+9% YoY), with Digital Operations growing 87% YoY [1]. The Delfi platform, AI-driven reservoir modeling, and Data Center Solutions represent meaningful and growing AI-adjacent revenue streams.

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Olivier Le Peuch demonstrates several hallmarks of infinite game thinking — the courageous rebranding from the 96-year-old Schlumberger name, strategic repositioning toward digital and energy transition, and commitment to Scope 3 emissions targets that most peers avoid. Le Peuch's vision of SLB as a 'global technology company' rather than an oilfield services firm reflects a genuine effort to redefine the company's identity for long-term relevance. However, the company's core business remains oil and gas services, and the transformation is still early.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): SLB's repositioned purpose — driving energy innovation for a balanced planet — is genuine and distinctive within the energy services sector. Le Peuch has consistently articulated a vision of dual energy access and climate challenges, and strategic decisions (rebranding, digital pivot, Scope 3 targets, Data Center Solutions) trace back to this purpose [2]. The purpose has guided the company through Le Peuch's 7-year tenure but hasn't yet been tested across a leadership transition or major crisis to reach the highest tier.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Le Peuch emphasizes 'collective creativity' and team empowerment, and the 109,000-employee global organization has maintained relative stability during the transformation period [2]. The company's multicultural, multinational workforce has operated collaboratively for nearly a century. However, SLB executed significant layoffs during the 2020 downturn, and the inherently cyclical nature of energy services creates periodic workforce disruptions that undermine long-term trust.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB competes with Halliburton and Baker Hughes but frames competition in terms of technology leadership and innovation rather than market share destruction. Le Peuch's strategic positioning — repositioning SLB as a technology company while competitors remain traditional oilfield services firms — demonstrates using rivals' positioning to clarify SLB's own differentiated path [2]. The company's competitive language focuses on building long-term capability rather than winning this quarter's contracts.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB and strategic pivot toward digital solutions, energy transition, and data center services represent meaningful flexibility — abandoning a 96-year-old brand name takes courage [2]. However, the company's core business remains oil and gas services (~90%+ of revenue), and the transformation is incremental rather than truly transformative. SLB has not abandoned its legacy business in the way that earns the highest score.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Le Peuch has made multiple courageous decisions — rebranding the 96-year-old Schlumberger name to SLB, committing to Scope 3 emissions targets (which most energy peers avoid), investing heavily in digital and Data Center Solutions before demand was proven, and acquiring ChampionX for approximately $8B to transform the production segment [1][2]. Setting a net-zero commitment including Scope 3 emissions in an oil services company is an act of genuine courage that accepts short-term criticism from both environmental advocates and fossil fuel supporters.

investment_thesis: SLB is the world's largest oilfield services company executing a strategic transformation into a technology-driven energy solutions provider. Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B (+3% YoY) was supported by Digital Operations growth of 87% and Data Center Solutions revenue of $141M (+45% YoY), demonstrating that the digital pivot is producing real results [1]. The company guided full-year 2026 revenue of $36.9-$37.7B with ~$4B+ in shareholder returns. At 17.3x forward earnings with a 2.04% dividend yield, 44 consecutive years of payments, and D/E of only 42.5%, SLB offers a combination of growth optionality and dividend reliability that is rare in energy services. The rebranding and ChampionX acquisition position SLB for the next decade of energy evolution [4].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on organic revenue weakness — excluding ChampionX, Q1 2026 revenue declined 7% YoY, indicating that the core oilfield services business is under pressure from softening international activity and Middle East geopolitical disruptions [1]. EPS declined 13.8% YoY, and the PEG ratio of 1.99 suggests the stock is expensive relative to near-term growth. Profit margin of 9.3% and ROE of 14.1% remain below the targets, and the payout ratio of 50.7% is elevated for a cyclical business. SLB's Scope 3 emissions commitment, while admirable, creates a delicate balancing act between serving traditional oil clients and positioning for the energy transition. If the energy supercycle softens, SLB's premium valuation relative to HAL and Baker Hughes leaves more room for multiple compression [4].
key_catalysts: Data Center Solutions revenue accelerating beyond $141M quarterly as AI infrastructure buildout drives demand for SLB's cooling, power, and facility management expertise in adjacent energy markets
Digital & Integration division sustaining 87% Digital Operations growth and expanding margins as SLB monetizes its proprietary subsurface data and AI-driven analytics platform at scale
ChampionX integration delivering targeted $400M+ in synergies by 2027, expanding the production segment and creating cross-selling opportunities across the combined customer base
key_risks: Core oilfield services revenue declining 7% YoY excluding ChampionX, indicating that organic growth is negative and the transformation has not yet offset the cyclical downturn in traditional activity
Middle East geopolitical disruptions creating revenue uncertainty in SLB's largest international market, with limited visibility on when activity levels will normalize
PEG ratio of 1.99 and trailing EPS decline of 13.8% suggest the stock is priced for transformation success that has yet to be demonstrated in the financial results
